title: From Solvers to Research: Large Language Model-Driven Formal Mathematics at the Research Frontier
authors: Eric Jiang, Xiao Liang, Yikai Zhang, Yingjia Wan, Mengting Li, Haikang Deng
abstract: Recent developments in AI for Mathematics (AI4Math), especially Large Language Model (LLM)-driven theorem provers, has achieved remarkable success in formal proof generation for well-defined mathematical problems through Interactive Theorem Proving (ITP) languages. However, current systems remain fundamentally limited in tackling frontier research mathematics, such as discovering new theorems or resolving open conjectures, which are often open-ended, under-specified, and involve multiple layers of abstraction. We argue that the next leap in AI4Math systems requires a decisive shift from predefined problem-solvers to research agents that can address frontier mathematical challenges with rigorous formal mathematical reasoning. In this position paper, we provide a systematic review of the field, covering datasets, auto-formalization, and proof synthesis. More importantly, we identify core limitations of existing systems in serving as mathematical research agents, examining issues across datasets, relational structure, mathematical exploration, tool ecosystem, and human-AI collaboration, outlining a strategic road-map for the future of AI4Math.

infographic.key_insight: LLM-based theorem provers are already strong at formal proof generation, but the paper argues they must evolve into research agents to tackle open-ended frontier mathematics.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For theorem-proving systems, the key design shift is from solving fixed benchmarks to supporting exploratory, tool-augmented, human-collaborative research workflows with formal rigor.
method: Systematically reviews the AI4Math stack: datasets, auto-formalization, and proof synthesis.
Analyzes limitations for research use across datasets, relational structure, mathematical exploration, tool ecosystem, and human-AI collaboration.
Outlines a roadmap for building frontier-capable mathematical research agents.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper is a position paper and review, so its evidence comes from synthesizing existing datasets, systems, and failure modes rather than presenting a new benchmark or quantitative result.
infographic.watch_item: A major implementation challenge is that frontier mathematics is open-ended and under-specified, making current solver-style LLMs brittle without stronger abstraction handling and interactive tooling.
infographic.paper_title: From Solvers to Research: Large Language Model-Driven Formal Mathematics at the Research Frontier

title: Understanding Schedule-Free Methods in Nonconvex Optimization: Rate Guarantees and Escaping Saddles
authors: Jiseok Chae, Donghwan Kim
abstract: Schedule-Free methods have attracted growing interest for alleviating the burden of designing and tuning a learning rate scheduler, while matching and sometimes even outperforming optimizers with tuned schedulers. Despite their strong empirical results, their convergence theory in nonconvex optimization, where modern machine learning objectives typically arise, has remained largely unexplored. In this paper, we provide worst-case analyses of Schedule-Free gradient descent and Schedule-Free stochastic gradient descent, in their standard form and without auxiliary modifications or restrictive conditions, for smooth but possibly nonconvex objectives. Based on a Lyapunov analysis derived from the continuous-time limiting ordinary differential equation associated with these methods, we show that Schedule-Free gradient descent and Schedule-Free stochastic gradient descent achieve the optimal worst-case convergence rates attainable among first-order methods. We further formulate Schedule-Free gradient descent as a nonautonomous dynamical system and prove strict-saddle avoidance under an arbitrarily small one-time perturbation. These theoretical results provide a better understanding of the strong performance that Schedule-Free methods demonstrate.

infographic.key_insight: Schedule-Free gradient methods can match the best possible worst-case convergence rates for smooth nonconvex optimization, while also avoiding strict saddles after a tiny perturbation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This suggests practitioners can reduce scheduler tuning overhead without sacrificing theoretical guarantees in challenging nonconvex machine learning objectives.
method: Lyapunov analysis from the continuous-time limiting ODE
Worst-case convergence proofs for Schedule-Free GD and SGD
Nonautonomous dynamical-system analysis for saddle avoidance
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper is a theory-focused analysis, deriving convergence and saddle-escape guarantees rather than reporting empirical benchmarks.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantees are worst-case and depend on smoothness assumptions; practical behavior may still depend on implementation details and stochastic noise conditions.
infographic.paper_title: Understanding Schedule-Free Methods in Nonconvex Optimization: Rate Guarantees and Escaping Saddles

title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory
authors: Jihao Liu, Guoxiong Gao, Zeming Sun, Bin Wu, Shurui Liu, Jiedong Jiang
abstract: Recent LLM-based mathematical reasoning agents have begun to tackle research-level problems and, in several cases, have contributed to the resolution of open problems. However, scaling and orchestrating such agents effectively remains challenging, due to the difficulty of coordinating parallel proof search while keeping intermediate claims organized and reliable. In this paper, we propose Danus, an orchestration system for research-level mathematical reasoning centered on a shared fact graph as a global memory-management mechanism. Danus consists of a main agent that performs planning and coordination, multiple worker agents that carry out proof search in parallel, and a stateless verifier that checks proposed mathematical claims before they are admitted into the fact graph. Each verified fact is stored together with its proof and logical dependencies, allowing the system to build long arguments incrementally while keeping the shared proof state organized. The main agent periodically summarizes the evolving proof state, redirects workers across promising directions, and supports interaction with human mathematicians through progress reports. We evaluate Danus through six research-level case studies in algebraic geometry, singularity theory, and combinatorics, illustrating how the fact-graph memory mechanism enables Danus to construct long, detailed mathematical proofs. Our results suggest that fact-graph-based orchestration provides an effective route toward scaling mathematical reasoning agents for long-horizon research problems. Danus is open source at https://github.com/frenzymath/Danus.

infographic.key_insight: Danus scales research-level mathematical reasoning by coordinating multiple proof-search agents through a shared fact graph that stores verified claims, proofs, and dependencies.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The system offers a practical multi-agent orchestration pattern for long-horizon theorem proving, combining planning, parallel search, verification, and memory management into a reusable workflow.
method: Main agent plans, summarizes progress, and redirects search.
Multiple worker agents explore proof ideas in parallel.
A stateless verifier admits only validated claims into the shared fact graph.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports six research-level case studies spanning algebraic geometry, singularity theory, and combinatorics, showing the approach can support long, detailed proofs.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend heavily on verifier reliability and on how well the fact graph stays consistent as proofs grow and workers explore many branches.
infographic.paper_title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory

title: Evaluating SageMath-Augmented LLM Agents for Computational and Experimental Mathematics
authors: Pavel Snopov, German Magai
abstract: Recent advances in AI for Mathematics have focused largely on autoformalization and theorem proving, leaving the role of Computer Algebra Systems (CAS) in agentic LLM workflows underexplored. We propose a ReAct-style agentic setup that combines LLM reasoning with verifiable feedback from SageMath, together with Context7 for the up-to-date documentation. We evaluate this agentic setup across frontier models for solving research-level mathematical problems from the RealMath benchmark in a setting that emulates a computational-mathematics research loop. We also propose a refinement to the RealMath benchmark by introducing a multi-step post-processing procedure and a multi-stage validation pipeline, both of which improve the quality and reliability of the extracted problem set. Our experiments reveal substantial performance gains from SageMath access across all evaluated models on +9.7~pp on average, the gains range from 1.5~pp to 27.8~pp and narrow the gap between open-weight and closed models. Qwen~3.7-Max benefits from SageMath the most, while GPT-5.5 achieves the highest solve rate of $75.2\%$ and the lowest token usage among tool-enabled configurations. Our findings suggest that CAS-augmented agents represent a promising direction for assisting mathematicians in computational exploration, and we believe that this work is a step towards automated conjecture discovery. The project repository is available online.

infographic.key_insight: Adding SageMath as a verifiable tool to LLM agents substantially improves performance on research-level mathematics tasks and narrows the gap between open and closed models.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This shows that CAS-augmented agent workflows can make computational math systems more accurate, efficient, and useful for iterative research and conjecture exploration.
method: Built a ReAct-style LLM agent that reasons step by step and calls SageMath for verified computation.
Used Context7 to fetch up-to-date documentation during tool use.
Refined the RealMath benchmark with multi-step post-processing and multi-stage validation to improve problem extraction quality.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across frontier models on the RealMath benchmark, SageMath access improved results by an average of +9.7 percentage points, with gains ranging from 1.5 to 27.8 points; GPT-5.5 reached 75.2% solve rate and the lowest token usage among tool-enabled setups.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on tool reliability and benchmark extraction quality, so agent gains may be sensitive to implementation details and validation pipeline choices.
infographic.paper_title: Evaluating SageMath-Augmented LLM Agents for Computational and Experimental Mathematics

title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
authors: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri
abstract: Current electroencephalography (EEG)-based dream detection relies on power spectral density (PSD) and statistical moment features, achieving a state-of-the-art area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of approximately 0.70 on the DREAM database (Wong et al., 2025, Nature Communications). We introduce PHINN-EEG (Persistent Homology Inspired Neural Network for EEG), the first topological time-series framework for dream mentation analysis. Using sliding-window Takens delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations on multichannel pre-awakening EEG epochs, we extract Dynamic Betti Curves that characterize the geometric architecture of neural activity, not merely its energy. These topological invariants, combined with topology-conditioned flow matching, are analytically projected to outperform existing PSD and catch22 benchmarks, targeting AUC = 0.82-0.90 on the 1,462-awakening open-access subset of the DREAM database (drawn from a full registry of 3,191 total awakenings from 263 participants across 20 independent laboratories). We further introduce a topology-conditioned rectified flow model for dream-state EEG synthesis-with a spectral-conditioned flow model of comparable feature dimensionality as an additional ablation baseline to isolate the value of topological conditioning specifically-and propose a set of candidate Betti transition archetypes linking topology to phenomenological dream report categories, presented as an exploratory hypothesis space pending empirical validation. If validated, this work represents a paradigm shift from spectral energy to phase-space geometry in neural rare-event detection, with potential future implications for wearable BCI dream monitoring.

infographic.key_insight: PHINN-EEG reframes dream-state EEG analysis from spectral features to topological dynamics, using Betti curve signatures from delay-embedded signals to classify dream mentation and guide EEG synthesis.
infographic.engineering_relevance: If effective, this could improve low-power, wearable dream-monitoring and rare-event EEG detection by adding phase-space geometry as a learnable signal descriptor beyond standard PSD features.
method: Sliding-window Takens delay embeddings on multichannel pre-awakening EEG
Vietoris-Rips persistent homology to extract Dynamic Betti Curves
Topology-conditioned rectified flow matching for signal synthesis, with a spectral-conditioned flow baseline for comparison
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract cites evaluation on the 1,462-awakening open-access DREAM subset from a larger 3,191-awakening, 263-participant, 20-lab registry, with a target AUC of 0.82–0.90 versus ~0.70 for prior PSD/statistical baselines.
infographic.watch_item: The topology-to-phenomenology links and the claimed performance gains are presented as analytically projected and exploratory, so empirical validation and careful control of preprocessing/windowing choices are essential.
infographic.paper_title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis

title: Characterization of the basin of convexity for multi-snapshot spike deconvolution via variable projection
authors: Meghna Kalra, Maxime Ferreira Da Costa, Kiryung Lee
abstract: We study the problem of multi-snapshot spike deconvolution, where the goal is to recover the locations of sparse impulses from their noisy convolution with a known point spread function (PSF) across multiple snapshots. We adopt a variable-projection formulation that eliminates the amplitudes in closed form, reducing the task to a nonconvex least-squares problem over the spike locations alone, which we refer to as the variable-projection formulation of spike deconvolution (VarProSD). We provide an explicit characterization of the basin of convexity of the VarProSD objective in terms of key PSF properties, including its power spectral density and smoothness, revealing how sampling bandwidth and spike separation influence the local geometry. Within this basin, we establish that the estimator is consistent in the number of snapshots under stochastic noise, and provide a complementary, sharper error bound under adversarial noise via the local Lipschitz property of the inverse map. We further show local convergence guarantees for gradient descent when initialized within the basin. A central ingredient throughout is the use of Beurling--Selberg extremal approximations, which enable sharp, PSF-agnostic bounds on the conditioning of the structured matrices arising in the optimization landscape. Numerical experiments validate our theoretical findings and demonstrate the effectiveness of modified ESPRIT initialization followed by gradient-based refinement.

infographic.key_insight: Variable projection turns multi-snapshot spike deconvolution into a smaller nonconvex problem over spike locations, and the paper characterizes exactly when that objective becomes locally convex and well-behaved.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives practical guidance for choosing bandwidth, snapshot count, and initialization so sparse signal recovery can be made reliable in imaging, microscopy, and spectral estimation pipelines.
method: Eliminate amplitudes in closed form to form the VarProSD objective over locations only.
Characterize the basin of convexity using PSF power spectrum, smoothness, and spike separation.
Use Beurling–Selberg extremal approximations to bound conditioning and prove local convergence/error guarantees.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments that validate the theory and show modified ESPRIT initialization followed by gradient refinement works well.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantees are local, so performance still depends on getting an initialization inside the basin of convexity.
infographic.paper_title: Characterization of the basin of convexity for multi-snapshot spike deconvolution via variable projection

title: Learning the Energy Landscapes of Dynamical Systems via Energetic Variational Optimal Transport under Data Quantity--Quality Trade-offs
authors: Shun Wang, Chun Liu, Wenrui Hao
abstract: Dynamic optimal transport unifies optimal transport, fluid mechanics, and gradient-flow theory within a continuous dynamical framework, offering a geometry-aware language for applications across physics, biology, and machine learning. However, conventional formulations cast it as a constrained optimization problem that must explicitly satisfy the continuity equation, hindering the reconstruction of the underlying dynamics directly from data. We propose the energetic variational method for dynamic optimal transport (EVMDOT), which reformulates it within an energetic variational framework by combining the flow map, the least action principle, and the maximum dissipation principle. The flow map recasts the constrained problem as an unconstrained one by automatically enforcing the continuity equation, while the balance between the conservative and dissipative forces determines the velocity field. Applied to the Fokker--Planck equation, the EVMDOT reconstructs both the energy landscape and the Waddington landscape directly from time-series density data. Through numerical experiments, we reveal that the EVMDOT achieves an intrinsic balance between data quantity and data quality: a sufficient data quantity compensates for limited data quality, making the reconstruction robust to the choice of the observation window. We further apply the EVMDOT to the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset to infer the potential landscape of amyloid-$β$ and tau, revealing two wells corresponding to the cognitively normal and Alzheimer's disease stages and the transition pathway between them.

infographic.key_insight: EVMDOT turns dynamic optimal transport into an unconstrained energetic variational problem that can recover both velocity fields and energy landscapes directly from density time-series data.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a data-driven way to infer latent dynamics and potential landscapes in complex systems, with applications in physics-informed modeling, biology, and medical trajectory analysis.
method: Uses the flow map to automatically satisfy the continuity equation.
Combines least action and maximum dissipation principles to balance conservative and dissipative forces.
Applies the framework to Fokker–Planck dynamics to reconstruct energy and Waddington landscapes from observed densities.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper reports numerical experiments showing robust landscape reconstruction across data quantity–quality trade-offs and an application to ADNI that separates cognitively normal and Alzheimer's disease states with a transition path.
infographic.watch_item: Reconstruction quality still depends on the amount and reliability of time-series density data, so sparse or noisy observations may limit identifiability.
infographic.paper_title: Learning the Energy Landscapes of Dynamical Systems via Energetic Variational Optimal Transport under Data Quantity--Quality Trade-offs

title: A Formalization of the Mean-Field Derivation of the Vlasov Equation: AI-Assisted Lean Formalization as a Strategy Game
authors: Joseph K. Miller
abstract: We formalize a research result in the Lean 4 proof assistant by having a mathematician direct an AI system, and frame the activity as a formalization game. The objective is to turn a LaTeX document into Lean. The game is won when the development compiles, contains no sorry, and a machine check shows the target theorems rest on Lean's foundational axioms alone. Reuse is a second check, by a definition we introduce: whether the development yields a self-contained layer of general mathematics the wider library could absorb. The case study is a complete, axiom-clean formalization of well-posedness for the nonlinear Vlasov equation via Dobrushin's mean-field route -- existence, uniqueness, the stability estimate and mean-field limit, and a short-window superposition principle (weak solutions are Lagrangian). The human's role was to direct, not to write proofs: to scope the definitions, steer the decompositions, and triage the library's gaps; the AI agent executed. The formalization certifies the proof of each statement as written; whether the written statement is the intended theorem stays the mathematician's judgment. The optimal-transport machinery that fell out of the build (in particular, properties of the Wasserstein-1 metric and the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality theorem) separates into a self-contained layer that compiles against Mathlib alone: about a sixth of the development (49 of 299 declarations), behind a 22-declaration interface with no reverse dependency. The headline theorems ran in about a week, the full development in about a month. We report the quantitative claims as observations of one game, not as general laws. The game's rules name no particular system, so the methodological framing is meant to outlast the tools of any one run.

infographic.key_insight: A mathematician-plus-AI workflow can produce a fully axiom-clean Lean 4 formalization of a major mean-field result for the Vlasov equation, including existence, uniqueness, stability, and the mean-field limit.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The paper suggests AI-assisted theorem proving can accelerate formal verification while also generating reusable mathematical libraries, especially in areas like optimal transport and PDE analysis.
method: Directed AI-assisted translation from LaTeX to Lean 4
Formalization game criteria: compile successfully, no `sorry`, and rely only on Lean's foundation
Reused the resulting optimal-transport layer as a self-contained Mathlib-compatible module
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a complete development with 299 declarations, 49 reusable declarations, a 22-declaration interface, and runtime of about a week for headline theorems and about a month for the full build.
infographic.watch_item: The results come from one case study, so the reported speed and reuse gains may not generalize across papers, toolchains, or theorem domains.
infographic.paper_title: A Formalization of the Mean-Field Derivation of the Vlasov Equation: AI-Assisted Lean Formalization as a Strategy Game

title: Nonconvex Composite Functional Constraints via First-Order Augmented Lagrangian Methods under Local Regularity
authors: Linglingzhi Zhu, Jiajin Li
abstract: We study nonasymptotic convergence of primal-dual methods for a class of nonconvex constrained optimization problems with a convex-composite structure. In this class, both the objective and the functional inequality constraints are given by convex Lipschitz outer functions composed with smooth nonlinear inner mappings. The analysis is complicated by constraint violation in a nonconvex functional inequality system and by the lack of an a priori bound on the multipliers. To address these issues, we restrict the dual variable to an auxiliary compact set and analyze a smoothed prox-linear augmented Lagrangian method through a nonsmooth nonconvex-concave minimax reformulation. The main contribution is a finite-time mechanism for converting stationarity of the truncated minimax problem into a KKT certificate for the original constrained problem. We show that, for a sufficiently large penalty parameter, all but a controlled number of iterates enter a near-feasible region. On this region, a local conic regularity condition uniformly bounds the associated prox-linear multipliers and thereby makes the artificial dual truncation inactive at the selected iterates. Building on this mechanism, we establish explicit convergence rates for the proposed method in terms of the KKT residual. With dual regularization, a global dual error bound together with a bias-balancing argument gives an $O(K^{-1/3})$ rate. In the unregularized case, under additional local structural assumptions including piecewise linearity of the outer functions, a local dual error bound yields the sharper $O(K^{-1/2})$ rate.

infographic.key_insight: A truncated, smoothed prox-linear augmented Lagrangian framework can turn stationarity of a nonconvex minimax surrogate into a finite-time KKT certificate for constrained problems with convex-composite objectives and functional inequality constraints.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives optimization practitioners a principled way to solve difficult constrained nonconvex models in PDE-constrained and other engineering settings while controlling feasibility and multiplier blow-up.
method: Restrict dual variables to a compact set and solve a smoothed prox-linear augmented Lagrangian reformulation.
Use local conic regularity to show the artificial dual truncation becomes inactive near feasible iterates.
Derive explicit KKT residual rates: O(K^-1/3) with dual regularization and O(K^-1/2) under stronger local structural assumptions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical nonasymptotic analysis and explicit convergence rates, but no numerical experiments or empirical dataset are described.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on sufficiently large penalty parameters and local regularity/structural assumptions, so the guarantees may be sensitive to problem-specific verification of these conditions.
infographic.paper_title: Nonconvex Composite Functional Constraints via First-Order Augmented Lagrangian Methods under Local Regularity

title: Mathematical methods of reinforcement learning
authors: Denis Belomestny, Alexander Gasnikov, Egor Gladin, Alexey Naumov, Artemy Rubtsov, Yuri Sapronov
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly grounded in tools from probability, optimization, and operator theory. This survey organizes the mathematical structures that underpin the design and analysis of modern algorithms in RL. We begin from Markov decision processes (MDPs) and the Bellman operators, emphasizing contraction mappings, monotonicity, and fixed-point theory that yield convergence guarantees and rates for value and policy iteration, and temporal-difference schemes. We then develop the optimization perspective: stochastic approximation and martingale methods, convex duality and the role of regularization linking mirror/proximal methods. Function approximation is treated through linear and non-linear settings, covering stabilization, error decomposition, and sample-complexity via concentration inequalities for dependent data and mixing processes. We further cover off-policy evaluation/learning, constrained RL and constrained MDPs (CMDPs). Throughout we unify algorithmic templates under common operator and variational lenses, highlighting both finite-sample bounds and asymptotic results. Our presentation is intended to provide a unified mathematical entry point for researchers in probability, optimization, and statistics interested in reinforcement learning.

infographic.key_insight: This survey shows that reinforcement learning can be analyzed as a unified problem in operator theory, stochastic optimization, and probability, with Bellman fixed-point structure as the central mathematical backbone.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It gives practitioners a principled way to predict convergence, stability, and sample efficiency of RL algorithms, especially when using function approximation, off-policy data, or constraints.
method: Bellman operators and fixed-point theory for value/policy iteration and temporal-difference methods
Stochastic approximation, martingale tools, and convex duality for optimization-based RL analysis
Linear and nonlinear function approximation with regularization, concentration bounds, and mixing-process arguments
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper is a survey rather than an experiment-driven study, so its evidence is a synthesis of finite-sample bounds and asymptotic results from the RL literature.
infographic.watch_item: Theoretical guarantees may weaken under nonlinear approximation, correlated data, or practical off-policy settings, so implementation stability can depend heavily on model and data assumptions.
infographic.paper_title: Mathematical methods of reinforcement learning

title: Any-Dimensional Learning by Sampling
authors: Eitan Levin, Venkat Chandrasekaran
abstract: Many machine learning models are defined for inputs of different sizes, such as point clouds containing different numbers of points, sequences of tokens of different lengths, and graphs on different numbers of nodes. Such models are trained on finitely-many examples of necessarily limited sizes. How well do these models generalize from inputs of small size to larger inputs of size not seen during training? Furthermore, evaluating such models on large inputs is often expensive. How can we sketch large inputs to obtain smaller ones on which the model takes similar values? At the heart of both questions is the need to compare inputs of different sizes and to approximate large inputs by small ones. We present a unified approach to address these questions by using random sampling maps to compare inputs of different sizes. The sampling maps we consider are generalizations of sampling with replacement, random binning, and species sampling. We characterize the application domains in which each type of sampling is appropriate in terms of the symmetries and relations between problem instances of different sizes in the domain. Our framework yields explicit generalization and sketching rates for function classes continuous with respect to a chosen notion of sampling, encompassing large families of functions defined on sequences, graphs, and tensors of different sizes. Specific examples include moment polynomials on measures, homomorphism densities and numbers of graphs, permutation-invariant transformers, and graph neural networks.

infographic.key_insight: Random sampling maps provide a unified way to compare and compress inputs of different sizes, enabling both cross-size generalization analysis and efficient sketching for models on sequences, graphs, tensors, and point clouds.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives practitioners a principled framework to estimate when a model trained on small inputs should transfer to larger ones, and when large inputs can be safely downsampled for cheaper inference.
method: Define size-to-size comparison via randomized sampling maps rather than direct input matching.
Classify when sampling with replacement, random binning, or species sampling is appropriate based on domain symmetries.
Derive explicit generalization and sketching rates for sampling-continuous function classes.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper is theoretical: the abstract reports explicit rates and examples across measures, graphs, permutation-invariant transformers, and graph neural networks, rather than an empirical benchmark study.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantees depend on the chosen sampling notion and continuity assumptions, so a real deployment must verify that the target model and data domain match the framework's symmetry structure.
infographic.paper_title: Any-Dimensional Learning by Sampling

title: Higher-Order Geometric Updates for Levenberg-Marquardt Method via Riemann Normal Coordinates
authors: Jianing Liu, Dong H. Zhang
abstract: Nonlinear least-squares optimization is central to regression, physics-informed neural networks, and other machine-learning tasks. Such problems have a natural geometric interpretation, model predictions form a manifold in data space, while the chosen parameterization can introduce parameter-effects curvature that becomes a dominant source of nonlinearity. This exposes a limitation of the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) method, its tangent-space step is applied as a straight update in parameter coordinates. Geodesic acceleration gives a second-order correction, but its removal of parameter-effect curvature is exact only in the infinitesimal-step limit. We propose a Riemann-normal-coordinate Levenberg-Marquardt method (RNC-LM) to improve this consistency for finite optimization steps. By reformulating the geodesic equation, RNC-LM extends geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections and constructs finite-step updates with progressively higher reparameterization consistency. A line search along the resulting RNC curve controls the traveled distance while keeping the cost close to standard LM. The method eliminates the tangential component of residual acceleration order by order in a moving tangent frame, making the actual objective reduction more consistent with the linear model prediction of LM. On classical nonlinear least-squares benchmarks, RNC-LM improves convergence and robustness in curved valleys and rank-deficient problems. On a reaction-diffusion PINN failure-mode benchmark, it reduces the relative L2 error to the order of 1e-3 and recovers a physically meaningful solution. On a large-scale machine-learning potential-energy-surface fitting task, it achieves a 34-fold speedup over standard LM.

infographic.key_insight: RNC-LM improves Levenberg-Marquardt by using Riemann normal coordinates to add higher-order geometric corrections, making finite optimization steps more consistent with the underlying manifold geometry.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a more robust solver for curved, ill-conditioned nonlinear least-squares problems in applications such as PINNs, regression, and large-scale parameter fitting.
method: Reformulates the geodesic equation in Riemann normal coordinates to extend geodesic acceleration beyond second order.
Constructs finite-step updates that progressively remove parameter-effect curvature in a moving tangent frame.
Uses a line search along the RNC curve to control step length while staying close to standard LM cost behavior.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports better convergence and robustness on classical nonlinear least-squares benchmarks, a reaction-diffusion PINN failure case with relative L2 error around 1e-3, and a 34-fold speedup on a large-scale potential-energy-surface fitting task.
infographic.watch_item: The added geometric corrections and line-search machinery may increase implementation complexity and require careful handling to preserve the computational efficiency of LM.
infographic.paper_title: Higher-Order Geometric Updates for Levenberg-Marquardt Method via Riemann Normal Coordinates

title: Physics-Informed Neural Embeddings of PDE Solution Families
authors: Raul Jimenez, Svitlana Mayboroda, Pavlos Protopapas, Leonid Sarieddine, David N. Spergel, Pedro Tarancón-Álvarez
abstract: We introduce a physics-informed framework for learning finite-dimensional embeddings of solution families of partial differential equations. The method uses a multihead Physics-Informed Neural Network in which a shared body learns a latent manifold representing the solution space, while linear heads reconstruct individual solutions associated with different initial conditions. A head-orthogonalization penalty removes degeneracies in the latent representation and stabilizes the principal-component spectrum across training realizations. Because the initial condition is built into the network output by construction, these principal components measure the additional variability the network learns on top of the initial profile, not the full solution itself. We apply the method to the one-dimensional viscous Burgers equation, with the heat and wave equations as robustness checks. For a latent dimension $n_b=20$, the learned manifolds exhibit pronounced effective dimensional reduction: for Burgers dynamics, only $2$-$4$ principal components capture about $95\%$ of the latent-space variance, while $4$-$7$ capture about $99\%$, depending on the initial-condition family; the same qualitative compression holds for the heat and wave equations. We also split the wavenumber axis into bands (``Fourier shells'') and measure how much each band contributes to every principal component. The resulting frequency profile is invariant under the change-of-basis freedom that the orthogonalization penalty leaves in the latent space, and is therefore reproducible across independent training runs. More broadly, this establishes the learned spectral profiles and principal components as robust observables of solution-manifold geometry.

infographic.key_insight: A multihead physics-informed neural network can learn a low-dimensional latent manifold of PDE solution families, revealing robust principal directions and frequency structure beyond the initial condition.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a compact, reproducible way to model and analyze parameterized PDE solution spaces for surrogate simulation, reduced-order modeling, and spectral diagnostics.
method: Shared PINN body learns a latent embedding of the solution manifold while linear heads reconstruct solutions for different initial conditions.
A head-orthogonalization penalty removes latent degeneracies and stabilizes principal-component spectra across training runs.
Fourier-shell analysis attributes frequency-band contributions to each principal component in a basis-invariant way.
infographic.evidence_signal: On the 1D viscous Burgers equation, a latent dimension of 20 compressed to about 2–4 principal components for ~95% variance and 4–7 for ~99%, with similar qualitative results for heat and wave equations.
infographic.watch_item: Because the initial condition is built into the output by construction, the learned principal components describe only additional variability; results may also depend on the chosen orthogonalization strength and latent dimension.
infographic.paper_title: Physics-Informed Neural Embeddings of PDE Solution Families

title: Graph-Regularized Low-Rank Matrix Completion by Variable Projection
authors: Benoît Loucheur, P. -A. Absil, Michel Journée
abstract: We address the low-rank matrix completion problem by incorporating graph regularization into the existing Riemannian Trust-Region Matrix Completion (RTRMC) framework. The latter uses the geometry of the low-rank constraint to remodel the problem as an unconstrained optimization problem on a single Grassmann manifold. Our approach, named Graph-Regularized RTRMC (GR-RTRMC), exploits the inherent relationships between rows and columns of the matrix. By using these relationships, we aim to improve the accuracy and robustness of matrix completion, particularly in scenarios where the underlying data exhibits strong correlations between rows or columns.

infographic.key_insight: The paper adds graph-based row/column regularization to Riemannian low-rank matrix completion, improving the classical RTRMC formulation via variable projection on a Grassmann manifold.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This is relevant for recommender systems, sensor data recovery, and any application where missing entries can be inferred more reliably by exploiting known similarities between rows or columns.
method: Start from the RTRMC framework for low-rank matrix completion.
Introduce graph regularization to encode relationships among rows and columns.
Use variable projection to keep the optimization on a single Grassmann manifold.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract claims improved accuracy and robustness, especially when the data has strong row or column correlations, but it does not report specific experiments or numerical results.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality of the graph structure; if the similarity graph is inaccurate or weak, regularization may bias the completion result.
infographic.paper_title: Graph-Regularized Low-Rank Matrix Completion by Variable Projection

title: Towards Detecting Inconsistencies in End-to-end Generated TODs
authors: Tiziano Labruna, Giovanni Bonetta, Bernardo Magnini
abstract: Generative AI is profoundly transforming the core technologies behind conversational systems, shifting from component-based to end-to-end approaches. However, Large Language Models (LLMs) may still generate inconsistencies, a critical issue particularly in Task-Oriented Dialogues (TODs), where system responses must strictly adhere to information from a domain knowledge base (e.g., restaurants in a city). A single hallucination (e.g., suggesting a non-existent restaurant) can lead to severe task failures. We investigate a method for automatically detecting inconsistencies by conceptualizing TODs as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP), where variables represent dialogue segments referencing the conversational domain, and constraints among variables capture dialogue properties such as turn coherence and adherence to domain knowledge. We propose a pipeline that first identifies variables in a target dialogue and then applies a CSP solver to identify valid solutions. By comparing the target dialogue with valid variable assignments, we can detect inconsistencies and suggest minimal changes to ensure dialogue consistency. We demonstrate the high accuracy of the CSP-based approach in detecting inconsistencies, and provide a detailed analysis of our findings.

infographic.key_insight: The paper reframes task-oriented dialogue inconsistency detection as a constraint satisfaction problem, enabling automatic identification of hallucinations and other violations against domain knowledge.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives conversational AI systems a principled way to validate end-to-end generated responses before deployment, improving reliability in high-stakes service workflows.
method: Identify dialogue segments that act as CSP variables.
Encode dialogue coherence and domain adherence as constraints.
Use a CSP solver to find valid assignments and compare them with the generated dialogue to flag inconsistencies and suggest minimal fixes.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a detailed evaluation showing high accuracy for the CSP-based inconsistency detection approach.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on correctly extracting dialogue variables and constraints, which may be challenging in noisy or ambiguous real-world conversations.
infographic.paper_title: Towards Detecting Inconsistencies in End-to-end Generated TODs

title: OpenProver: Agentic and Interactive Theorem Proving with Lean 4
authors: Matěj Kripner, Milan Straka
abstract: In this system paper, we present OpenProver, an open-source system for LLM-driven automated theorem proving (ATP) with integrated Lean 4 formal verification. OpenProver integrates a Planner-Worker-Verifier architecture inspired by recent ATP agentic systems such as Aletheia. A Planner agent maintains a compact Whiteboard scratchpad and an unbounded Repository of intermediate findings, and decomposes mathematical work into parallel Workers. OpenProver is fully open-source, offers reproducible evaluation through automatic formal verification of generated proofs, and provides an interactive terminal interface for human-guided proof search. In interactive mode, OpenProver allows the human operator to monitor and steer the proof search process, motivated by the established human-AI synergy in interactive code generation. To showcase the potential for quantitative ablation experiments enabled by automatic formal verification, we evaluate OpenProver on ProofNet and compare it with a simple baseline. OpenProver is publicly available at https://github.com/kripner/OpenProver.

infographic.key_insight: OpenProver combines LLM-driven planning, parallel worker decomposition, and Lean 4 verification to make automated theorem proving more reproducible and interactive.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides an open-source, human-guided ATP workflow that can support safer experimentation, ablation studies, and proof search tooling for formal methods teams.
method: Planner maintains a compact Whiteboard and an unbounded Repository for intermediate results.
Planner decomposes proof tasks into parallel Workers for concurrent exploration.
Verifier automatically checks generated Lean 4 proofs, with an interactive terminal for human steering.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper evaluates OpenProver on ProofNet and compares it against a simple baseline, using automatic formal verification to enable reproducible quantitative ablations.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on Lean 4 verification overhead and the quality of human or planner guidance during interactive proof search.
infographic.paper_title: OpenProver: Agentic and Interactive Theorem Proving with Lean 4

title: Dynamics of Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Near a Manifold of Flat Minima
authors: Lachlan Ewen MacDonald, René Vidal
abstract: An important quantity in the theory of gradient descent (GD) is the \emph{sharpness}, defined as the largest eigenvalue of the objective Hessian. Classical analyses typically require the step size to be uniformly smaller than twice the reciprocal of the sharpness, but this condition is frequently violated in the training of deep neural networks. Recent work bridges this gap in the setting of overparametrised least-squares with a \emph{single scalar output}, providing a normal form for large-step GD in a neighbourhood of an \emph{isolated} flat minimum and establishing three corresponding convergence results. In this paper, we extend this theory in two directions: (1) to overparametrised least-squares with \emph{vector-valued outputs} (including regression with arbitrarily many observations), and (2) to a neighbourhood of a \emph{manifold} of flat minima (which we show is essential for applications such as matrix factorisation). We generalise both the normal form and all three convergence theorems of \cite{macdonaldeos} to this broader setting, overcoming several technical challenges, including the solution of a singular partial differential equation via a novel method that may be of independent interest. We further show that our framework applies to deep matrix factorisation under mild assumptions, yielding several new structural results. In particular, we prove that the set of flat minima forms a fibre bundle over a product of spheres, and that the sharpness is Morse-Bott along this manifold.

infographic.key_insight: The paper extends large-step gradient descent theory from isolated flat minima to vector-output least-squares and to entire manifolds of flat minima, while preserving a normal form and convergence guarantees.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a more realistic convergence framework for overparameterized models and matrix factorization, where step sizes can exceed the classic sharpness-based limit near flat solution sets.
method: Generalize the large-step GD normal form from scalar to vector-valued outputs.
Replace isolated minima with a manifold-of-minima setting and analyze local dynamics near this set.
Handle the resulting singular PDE with a new solution method, then apply the framework to deep matrix factorization.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports three extended convergence theorems, a new normal form, and structural results for deep matrix factorization, but does not mention experiments or numerical benchmarks.
infographic.watch_item: The results are local and rely on mild assumptions plus a manifold structure, so practical use may depend on verifying these geometric conditions in a given model.
infographic.paper_title: Dynamics of Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Near a Manifold of Flat Minima

title: Reinforcing the Generation Order of Multimodal Masked Diffusion Models
authors: Yidong Ouyang, Zhe Wang, Sourav Bhabesh, Dmitriy Bespalov
abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have recently achieved substantial progress in natural language generation tasks. Recent research demonstrates that adaptive token generation ordering can significantly improve performance in mathematical reasoning and code synthesis applications. In this work, we investigate the optimization of generation order for both text-to-image synthesis and multimodal understanding. We first establish that, unlike structured problems in language generation such as Sudoku puzzles, model logits alone are insufficient for determining optimal generation sequences in text-to-image generation and multimodal understanding. To address this challenge, we introduce a learnable control module trained via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to determine the generation order. Our results demonstrate that learning this control block substantially improves both text-to-image alignment and multimodal understanding in DLMs. In particular, it enhances the model's ability to capture fine-grained spatial relationships in generated images while also strengthening performance on multimodal reasoning and comprehension tasks. We evaluate our framework on GenEval, an object-focused benchmark for text-to-image alignment, where it achieves 4.08% relative improvements. In addition, experiments on VLMEvalKit confirm 4.85% relative improvements in multimodal understanding, highlighting the broad effectiveness of our approach.

infographic.key_insight: Learned generation ordering, rather than logits alone, can materially improve multimodal masked diffusion models for both image alignment and multimodal reasoning.
infographic.engineering_relevance: A small control module trained with GRPO offers a practical way to steer diffusion-based generators toward better spatial consistency and comprehension without redesigning the base model.
method: Train a learnable control block to predict generation order
Optimize the controller with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)
Apply the learned order to text-to-image synthesis and multimodal understanding tasks
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports 4.08% relative improvement on GenEval for text-to-image alignment and 4.85% relative improvement on VLMEvalKit for multimodal understanding.
infographic.watch_item: The method adds an extra learned control component, so its benefit may depend on stable GRPO training and may increase system complexity at inference or deployment.
infographic.paper_title: Reinforcing the Generation Order of Multimodal Masked Diffusion Models

title: Trustworthy Machine Learning through the Lens of Combinatorial Optimization: Survey and Research Perspectives
authors: Thibaut Vidal, Julien Ferry
abstract: Modern machine learning (ML) increasingly relies on complex models whose behavior is difficult to characterize beyond empirical performance metrics. Across a wide range of tasks, including prediction, generation, and decision-making, models with similar empirical performance can exhibit markedly different properties in terms of their transparency, interpretability, robustness, fairness, privacy, and certifiability. This survey highlights how optimization- and certification-oriented reasoning can provide a useful framework for reasoning about such differences, supporting tasks ranging from model training and selection to auditing and certification. We review and synthesize recent advances at the intersection of combinatorial optimization (CO) and trustworthy ML, covering both training and post-training tasks, including interpretable model learning, explanation generation, robustness analysis, fairness auditing, model compression, and privacy attacks and protections. Across these domains, CO formulations offer additional capabilities over purely heuristic approaches, e.g., gradient-based ones, notably global guarantees, formal certificates, and explicit treatment of trade-offs. While scalability remains an important challenge, continued progress in solvers and hybrid algorithms suggests a growing role for CO in the design and deployment of trustworthy ML systems.

infographic.key_insight: Combinatorial optimization and certification methods can make machine learning more trustworthy by enabling global guarantees, formal certificates, and explicit trade-off analysis beyond standard heuristic training.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For deployed ML systems, this perspective supports safer model selection, auditing, compression, robustness checks, fairness analysis, and privacy protection with more verifiable behavior.
method: Formulate trustworthiness tasks as optimization problems rather than only gradient-based objectives.
Use exact or certificate-producing solvers for training and post-training analysis.
Combine optimization with hybrid algorithms to improve scalability while retaining guarantees where possible.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract synthesizes recent research across prediction, generation, and decision-making, emphasizing comparative analysis of methods and their properties rather than reporting a single dataset or benchmark result.
infographic.watch_item: A key limitation is scalability: exact optimization and certification can become expensive on large models or datasets, so practical use may require approximations or hybrid pipelines.
infographic.paper_title: Trustworthy Machine Learning through the Lens of Combinatorial Optimization: Survey and Research Perspectives

title: Bidirectional Elaborators à la Carte
authors: Andrew Slattery, Jonathan Sterling
abstract: Surface syntax in proof assistants like Rocq, Lean, Agda, and Idris is highly implicit, lacking many details that are needed for user-written code to denote precisely defined mathematical objects. Elaboration is an algorithm that accounts for these details by translating surface syntax to an explicit enough core syntax. The reliability and predictability of elaboration relies on several critical properties of the core type system, including decidability of judgemental equality and the injectivity of type constructors; these dependencies are witnessed in a concrete system by explicit calls to conversion checking and weak-head reduction subroutines. We introduce a dependently typed monadic domain specific language for the executable specification of correct-by-construction elaboration algorithms that is abstracted from any particular representation of normal forms or algorithm for conversion checking. In particular, we represent a bidirectionally typed surface language for Martin-Löf type theory by shallow embedding in this DSL so that the translation of surface terms into core terms amounts to elementary equational calculation. This translation is correct by construction in the sense that it cannot produce ill-typed terms, and is automatically stable under judgemental equality of core terms and even under substitution; from the latter property, we obtain a new denotational interpretation of the suspension of elaboration problems. Finally, a concrete elaboration algorithm is extracted by algebraic means from a presheaf model of the DSL built out of the bi-initial natural model of Martin-Löf type theory.

infographic.key_insight: The paper gives a correct-by-construction, bidirectional elaboration framework that translates implicit proof-assistant syntax into explicit core terms without producing ill-typed output.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers a principled way to build reliable elaborators for theorem provers, reducing dependence on ad hoc conversion-checking and normalization machinery.
method: Defines a dependently typed monadic DSL for executable elaboration specifications
Shallowly embeds a bidirectionally typed surface language for Martin-Löf type theory into the DSL
Extracts a concrete elaboration algorithm algebraically from a presheaf model based on the bi-initial natural model
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a formal correctness result and an extracted concrete algorithm, but does not mention empirical benchmarks or experimental evaluation.
infographic.watch_item: The approach is mathematically elegant but may be challenging to implement efficiently, especially because it abstracts away the concrete representation of normal forms and conversion checking.
infographic.paper_title: Bidirectional Elaborators à la Carte
